network.overall_strategy: This network models glycine as the most accessible amino acid in prebiotic chemistry by centering the literature-supported hub molecules HCN, formaldehyde, ammonia, aminoacetonitrile, and glyoxylate. The dominant route is canonical Strecker synthesis from formaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN to aminoacetonitrile, followed by hydrolysis to glycinamide and glycine. Convergent alternatives include the Bucherer-Bergs detour through hydantoin and N-carbamoylglycine, HCN polymer and frozen ammonium cyanide reservoir chemistry that releases glycine upon hydrolysis, cyanosulfidic photochemistry that produces aminoacetonitrile directly, hydrothermal serpentinization-driven C1 generation feeding surface Strecker chemistry, ferroan brucite-mediated glyoxylate plus nitrate synthesis of glycine, and UV-driven reversible amination of glyoxylate. Hydrothermal reactions are restricted to experimentally supported CO2 reduction products and glyoxylate-mediated glycine synthesis, avoiding unsupported pyruvate-to-glyoxylate bridges. Surface reactions explicitly include mineral agents and use the exact environmental logic from the literature: HCN concentration in eutectic/frozen or evaporitic settings, UV photochemistry on TiO2, borate-assisted formaldehyde stabilization, and sulfide-mineral-supported cyanosulfidic chemistry. Biochemical/prebiotic reactions complete slow hydrolyses and peptide assembly.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich serpentinization setting

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 yields formate as a primary C1 product on Fe-S and Fe-oxide catalysts.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) showed greigite and magnetite catalyze CO2 + H2 to formate under alkaline hydrothermal conditions at 100C; this is a key hydrothermal feedstock node for downstream glycine chemistry.

reactions.name: Surface generation and concentration of formaldehyde from reduced C1 feedstocks

conditions.other: UV-exposed shallow pools; photochemical oxidation-reduction C1 network; borate stabilizes carbonyl products

reactions.mechanism: Hydrothermal C1 products are transferred to surface photochemical settings where formaldehyde is sustained as part of the reduced carbonyl pool and stabilized in borate-bearing evaporitic systems.
reactions.reasoning: The literature atlas identifies CO2 reduction to C1 compounds as optimal in alkaline vents and formaldehyde as a master precursor for Strecker chemistry; TiO2 is a documented prebiotic photocatalyst and borate minerals stabilize reactive sugars/carbonyl chemistry (Senanayake & Idriss, 2006; Patel et al., 2015). This node is a connecting availability step rather than a claim of a single exclusive mechanism.

reactions.name: Atmospheric and surface HCN supply

conditions.other: UV/lightning-fed atmospheric deposition into ponds; CO-rich or transiently reducing chemistry; surface concentration after deposition

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical and atmospheric energy sources generate cyanide-bearing feedstocks that accumulate in surface pools for prebiotic nitrile chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1955, 1957), Schlesinger & Miller (1983), Johnson et al. (2008), and Parker et al. (2011) support HCN formation in spark-discharge systems feeding Strecker chemistry. The network represents HCN as an available surface hub accumulated from atmosphere and energetic surface processing rather than claiming selective direct conversion from formaldehyde alone.

reactions.name: Surface ammonia availability from atmospheric and photochemical sources

conditions.other: UV-irradiated ponds and atmospheric deposition; buffered near-neutral to mildly alkaline conditions

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical and geochemical nitrogen cycling supplies ammonia to surface aqueous environments used in Strecker and cyanosulfidic chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report identifies ammonia production in atmosphere or on mineral surfaces as a necessary feedstock for glycine synthesis and notes nitrate stability in some scenarios; TiO2 is a documented prebiotic photocatalyst and phosphate-buffered conditions are central to cyanosulfidic chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Senanayake & Idriss, 2006). This is a conservative feedstock-availability step.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation: formaldehyde plus HCN to glycolonitrile

conditions.other: pH about 8; realistic prebiotic concentrations near 1 g/L HCN and HCHO

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide adds to formaldehyde to form glycolonitrile, a dominant early product and branch point in cyanide chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) found that at pH 8 and 25C with realistic HCN/HCHO concentrations, glycolonitrile is the major product (99.9%), making it a central branch point preceding glycine-producing chemistry.

reactions.name: Imine formation from formaldehyde and ammonia via aminomethanol

conditions.other: aqueous pond chemistry; dehydration-concentration equilibria

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia attacks formaldehyde to give aminomethanol, which dehydrates to methanimine, the reactive imine accepted in Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) mapped the Strecker free-energy landscape and identified aminomethanol and methanimine-type intermediates in aqueous glycinonitrile formation; this explicit mechanistic decomposition avoids over-compressing the dominant route.

reactions.name: Canonical Strecker cyanide addition to methanimine giving aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: warm pond or evaporitic pool chemistry; rate-limiting barrier about 15 kcal/mol for glycinonitrile formation

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide adds to methanimine to produce aminoacetonitrile, the direct nitrile precursor to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Miller (1953, 1957) identified Strecker chemistry as central to glycine formation, and Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) quantified glycinonitrile formation with a 15 kcal/mol barrier in water. Aminoacetonitrile is the dominant direct precursor emphasized throughout the literature report.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide

conditions.other: slow aqueous hydrolysis in concentrated prebiotic pools; nitrile hydration barrier about 23 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile of aminoacetonitrile hydrates through amide/imide-like intermediates to glycinamide.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) showed nitrile hydration of aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide is a key kinetic bottleneck with about 23 kcal/mol barrier. Representing glycinamide explicitly follows the literature and avoids collapsing hydrolysis into one step.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glycinamide to glycine

conditions.other: extended hydrolysis in pools or interfaces; final barrier about 31 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis converts glycinamide to glycine as the terminal step of the canonical Strecker route.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) identified the final amide hydrolysis to glycine as the highest barrier in the aqueous Strecker sequence, about 31 kcal/mol, making this the rate-limiting completion step.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs capture of aminoacetonitrile by CO2 to hydantoin

conditions.other: room temperature, bicarbonate-rich or CO2-rich aqueous setting

reactions.mechanism: CO2 captures aminoacetonitrile in a productive detour to cyclic hydantoin under Bucherer-Bergs-like conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) showed CO2 shifts minor aminoacetonitrile product into hydantoin under realistic prebiotic conditions, and Pulletikurti et al. (2022) demonstrated high hydantoin yields in bicarbonate-buffered prebiotic systems. This is the key alternative branch from the Strecker hub.

reactions.name: Hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoylglycine

conditions.other: prolonged aqueous hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening hydrolysis of hydantoin yields N-carbamoylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) describe hydantoin as the productive CO2-captured intermediate en route to glycine via N-carbamoylglycine in Bucherer-Bergs chemistry.

reactions.name: N-carbamoylglycine hydrolysis to glycine

conditions.other: continued hydrolysis in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Carbamoyl cleavage releases glycine from N-carbamoylglycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) established the hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine to glycine sequence as a realistic prebiotic alternative to direct nitrile hydrolysis.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to aminomalononitrile and DAMN reservoir

conditions.other: requires concentrated HCN above about 0.01-0.1 M or eutectic freezing near -23.4C; base-catalyzed polymerization

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes through aminomalononitrile and DAMN into heterogeneous polymer reservoirs.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966, 1967) quantified HCN oligomerization and its concentration threshold; the literature report emphasizes that eutectic freezing or other concentration mechanisms are required. This reaction intentionally outputs an ensemble reservoir rather than falsely assigning a single fixed polymer chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN polymer reservoir to glycine-rich amino acid mixture

conditions.other: acid or base hydrolysis; glycine is the most abundant identified amino acid but product mixture is expected

reactions.mechanism: Heterogeneous HCN oligomer mixtures hydrolyze to amino acids, with glycine consistently dominant among quantified products.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) found glycine to be the most abundant amino acid under all 23 tested HCN polymer hydrolysis conditions; Oró's early ammoniacal HCN work and later studies support this reservoir route. This is explicitly represented as mixture-to-glycine-rich output rather than a falsely selective direct precursor step.

reactions.name: Freezing concentration of ammonium cyanide reservoir

conditions.other: ice eutectic concentration near -20 to -78C over extended times

reactions.mechanism: HCN and ammonia form ammonium cyanide, which is concentrated and preserved in frozen eutectic brines.
reactions.reasoning: Levy et al. (2000) detected glycine from frozen NH4CN after long storage; Menor-Salván & Marín-Yaseli (2012) and the literature report emphasize ice eutectics as a key concentration and protection mechanism for cyanide chemistry.

reactions.name: Frozen ammonium cyanide aging/polymerization to glycine upon thaw-hydrolysis

conditions.other: long-term frozen storage followed by thawing/hydrolysis; eutectic brine chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Ammonium cyanide in ice ages into reactive oligomeric species that release glycine during subsequent hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Levy et al. (2000) detected glycine from frozen NH4CN solutions stored for years to decades, demonstrating a distinct ice-mediated reservoir route to glycine.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photochemistry to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: UV light; H2S present as reductant; Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox network in the experimental system

reactions.mechanism: Unified cyanosulfidic photoredox chemistry homologates cyanide feedstocks and furnishes aminoacetonitrile together with nucleotide and lipid precursors.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated that HCN + H2S + UV in phosphate-buffered conditions yields amino acid, nucleotide, and lipid precursors simultaneously, with aminoacetonitrile as the glycine precursor. The mineral agents here represent the required sulfide and phosphate geochemical context.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal glyoxylate availability in serpentinite systems

conditions.other: serpentinizing alkaline conditions; glyoxylate treated as available substrate in ferroan-brucite mineral systems

reactions.mechanism: Serpentinization-associated mineral systems furnish and retain glyoxylate-class carbonyl substrates relevant to amino acid synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 PNAS ferroan brucite study specifically demonstrates glycine synthesis from nitrate + glyoxylate and strong mineral sequestration of product. This node is included as a hydrothermal glyoxylate availability reservoir consistent with the study's substrate framework, while avoiding unsupported pyruvate-to-glyoxylate conversion.

reactions.name: Ferroan brucite-mediated synthesis of glycine from nitrate and glyoxylate

conditions.other: anoxic mineral slurry; up to 97% of glycine partitions to mineral fraction

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reductive amination/reduction of glyoxylate using nitrate-derived nitrogen yields glycine that is strongly sorbed to the mineral phase.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 PNAS study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121) showed ferroan brucite mediates glycine synthesis from nitrate + glyoxylate with strong product sequestration; this is one of the most direct and best-supported non-Strecker glycine routes in the report.

reactions.name: UV-driven reversible amination of glyoxylate to glycine

conditions.other: UV illumination; alpha-keto acid amination analogue

reactions.mechanism: Photoexcited mineral surfaces mediate reversible amination between glyoxylate and glycine in the presence of ammonia.
reactions.reasoning: Wang et al. (2012) demonstrated reversible UV-driven amination of alpha-keto acids, including glyoxylate-glycine interconversion, on semiconductor minerals; Kitadai et al. (2017) further support photocatalytic mineral roles in glycine-related chemistry. This provides a surface counterpart to the ferroan-brucite route.

reactions.name: Formamide generation from HCN hydrolysis/photochemistry

conditions.other: UV photochemistry in surface waters

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes hydration/hydrolysis in photochemically active settings to form formamide, another versatile prebiotic nitrogen-carbonyl intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report notes UV photochemistry linking HCN, water, and formamide, and Senanayake & Idriss (2006) showed TiO2 photocatalyzes prebiotic organics from formamide. Here formamide is included as a documented branch from the HCN hub, not as a speculative direct glycine precursor.

reactions.name: Formamide ammonolysis to formamidine-like amidine reservoir

conditions.other: UV-assisted dehydrating surface microenvironments

reactions.mechanism: Reactive amidine-forming chemistry generates formamidine-class intermediates that can feed aminoacetonitrile formation networks.
reactions.reasoning: This step is more speculative than the canonical Strecker route but is included only as a connecting branch from the formamide reservoir to aminoacetonitrile-class chemistry; it is justified by broader prebiotic amidine chemistry associated with HCN/formamide systems (Bernstein et al., 2002; Elsila et al., 2007), and is marked as a secondary connector.

reactions.name: Amidine-assisted conversion to aminoacetonitrile

conditions.other: concentrating pond chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Reactive amidine/nitrile chemistry converges on aminoacetonitrile in formaldehyde-containing cyanide-rich systems.
reactions.reasoning: This is a conservative network-bridging abstraction grounded in the centrality of aminoacetonitrile as a stable reservoir precursor detected in astrochemical and prebiotic contexts (Belloche et al., 2008; Magrino et al., 2021). It is explicitly less direct than the canonical Strecker steps and serves to connect the HCN/formamide branch to the main hub.

reactions.name: Meteoritic and Fischer-Tropsch precursor delivery enriching HCN-formaldehyde-ammonia pool

conditions.catalyst: native iron and Fe-Ni-like metallic surfaces
conditions.other: CO-rich reduced gas chemistry on metallic catalyst surfaces; cooler zones allow downstream Strecker accumulation

reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type catalytic processing of reduced gases generates formaldehyde- and cyanide-bearing organics plus amines that feed surface glycine chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Yoshino, Hayatsu & Anders (1971) demonstrated amino acid synthesis including glycine on metallic catalysts under FTT-like conditions, and the literature report emphasizes that FTT chemistry supplies formaldehyde and HCN precursor pools rather than a single one-step glycine reaction. This reaction is framed accordingly to avoid the over-compression criticized in previous attempts.

reactions.name: Methylamine oxidation-state connection to HCN-derived nitrogen chemistry

conditions.other: UV-exposed pond margins

reactions.mechanism: Amine-bearing products from FTT or cometary delivery participate in further amidine/nitrile exchange chemistry that reconnects to the aminoacetonitrile hub.
reactions.reasoning: Altwegg et al. (2016) detected methylamine together with glycine precursors in cometary material, and the literature report emphasizes methylamine as part of the same precursor network. This branch is included as a feed-in reservoir connection, not a claim of exclusive direct synthesis.

reactions.name: Wet-dry condensation of glycine to glycylglycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on mineral interfaces or brief hydrothermal pulses; dimerization optimum near pH 9.8 and 140C

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative peptide bond formation links glycine monomers into dipeptides under cycling or high-temperature conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Sakata et al. (2010) found glycine dimerization optimum at pH 9.8 and 140C, while Huber & Wachtershauser (1998) and Imai et al. (1999) support prebiotic peptide formation under mineral and hydrothermal conditions. This downstream step demonstrates biochemical relevance of the synthesized glycine pool.

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker route
pathways.description: The dominant literature-supported pathway: formaldehyde and ammonia form methanimine, cyanide adds to give aminoacetonitrile, and sequential hydrolysis yields glycinamide then glycine.

pathways.name: Cyanohydrin-fed Strecker route via glycolonitrile branch
pathways.description: Formaldehyde and HCN first give glycolonitrile in high yield under realistic conditions; the same precursor pool then converges to aminoacetonitrile and glycine through the Strecker hub.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs detour from aminoacetonitrile
pathways.description: Aminoacetonitrile is trapped by CO2 as hydantoin, hydrolyzed to N-carbamoylglycine, and then to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer reservoir hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes in eutectic or concentrated settings to DAMN-rich polymer reservoirs whose hydrolysis yields glycine as the most abundant amino acid.

pathways.name: Frozen ammonium cyanide route
pathways.description: HCN and ammonia are concentrated as ammonium cyanide in frozen eutectic brines; prolonged aging and thaw-hydrolysis yields glycine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic photochemistry route
pathways.description: Unified HCN-H2S-UV-phosphate chemistry produces aminoacetonitrile directly, which is then hydrolyzed to glycine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formate to surface Strecker route
pathways.description: Serpentinization-driven CO2 reduction generates formate in vents; this feeds surface formaldehyde and cyanide chemistry, culminating in Strecker glycine synthesis.

pathways.name: Ferroan brucite glyoxylate-nitrate route
pathways.description: A direct hydrothermal route where glyoxylate and nitrate on ferroan brucite yield glycine with strong mineral sequestration.

pathways.name: UV amination of glyoxylate route
pathways.description: Surface UV-driven semiconductor catalysis aminates glyoxylate with ammonia to glycine, connecting hydrothermal carbonyl chemistry to surface photochemistry.

pathways.name: FTT precursor-pool to Strecker route
pathways.description: Fischer-Tropsch-type metallic catalysis supplies formaldehyde, HCN, and amine-rich precursors that feed the canonical Strecker sequence to glycine.
